retrieved_docs.title: Vitiligo -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: Psoralen photochemotherapy; adverse effects include phototoxic effect, nausea, and risk of skin cancer. Short-term adverse effects of NB-UVB include itching, burning, and xerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Sunscreens and Photoprotection -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Adverse effects of sunscreen primarily include 4 types of contact dermatitis—irritant, allergic, phototoxic, and photoallergic. [23] In Germany, a 15-year study involving patch and photo patch testing found that a nonimmune-based irritant response is the most common reaction to sunscreen. [2] Benzophenones and dibenzoylmethanes are the most common UV filters that cause adverse effects, with benzophenone-3 (BP-3) being the most common photoallergen. As BP-3 is a derivative of PABA, it is not used frequently in the United States. [2] Some studies have associated sunscreen use with melanoma due to the users' false sense of security, which may increase the duration of the sun, resulting in UVA formation and malignant changes. [23] Phototoxic and allergic contact dermatitis are typically the results of UVA (320-400 nm) and visible light ranges (400-800 nm). UVA is capable of penetrating the reticular dermis and is the cause of most photosensitivity reactions. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Sunscreens and Photoprotection -- Mechanism of Action -- UVA Blockers
retrieved_docs.content: Typical benzophenones primarily absorb UVB; however, oxybenzone is considered a broad-spectrum absorber as it can also absorb UVA II. [20] Oxybenzone is the most commonly used benzophenone. [3] However, it is the most likely sunscreen ingredient to induce contact or photo-contact dermatitis. Oxybenzone is not considered photostable, and although it is not scientifically proven yet, there is a concern about carcinogenic and endocrine adverse effects. [20] Other FDA-listed benzophenones include sulisobenzone and dioxybenzone. [3] Anthranilates are weak UVB and UVA filters and are less effective than benzophenones. As a result, clinicians rarely use them. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroquinone -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Allergic reaction or hypersensitivity hydroquinone Sun exposure The use of this drug requires caution with other medications that cause photosensitivity.

retrieved_docs.title: Sunscreens and Photoprotection -- Mechanism of Action -- UVA Blockers
retrieved_docs.content: Broad-spectrum sunscreens include methylene bis-benzotriazolyl tetramethylbutylphenol (MBBT) and bis-ethylhexyloxyphenol methoxyphenyl triazine (BEMT). MBBT is advantageous due to its large molecular size, which decreases the likelihood of systemic absorption or endocrine effects. MBBT combines organic and inorganic filters, ultimately absorbing, scattering, and reflecting UV radiation. MBBT decreases UVA transmission more than UVB. BEMT is also photostable and does not interfere with the endocrine system. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Sunscreens and Photoprotection -- Mechanism of Action -- UVA Blockers
retrieved_docs.content: UVA blockers protect against long-wave ultraviolet radiation (320–400 nm), penetrating deeper into the skin than UVB and contributing to photoaging, pigmentation, and skin cancer. Benzophenones Anthranilates Avobenzone Ecamsule

retrieved_docs.title: Photodynamic Therapy for the Eye -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) usually involves the injection or topical application of a photosensitizing compound, which gets accumulated in the desired target cells, which are then irradiated by the light of a specific wavelength. [1] Hermann von Tappeiner, a professor from Germany, first coined the term 'photodynamic reaction' in the early 1900. It was preceded by a demonstration of the toxic effect of cumulative acridine orange (photosensitizer) on paramecia by his student Oscar Raab. The toxicity of acridine orange on paramecia was dependent on both concentration of the dye and the intensity of illumination.

retrieved_docs.title: Photosensitivity -- Introduction -- Exogenous or drug/chemical-induced photodermatoses [7] [8]
retrieved_docs.content: Drug-induced photosensitivity: common photosensitizing drugs are thiazides, tetracyclines, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), phenothiazines, voriconazole, quinine, vemurafenib, and many others [7] Photocontact dermatitis: due to phototoxic chemicals such as psoralens in plants, vegetables, fruit; fragrances in cosmetics; sunscreen chemicals; dyes and disinfectants [9] Pseudoporphyria: induced by drugs and/or renal insufficiency [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Mercaptan Toxicity -- History and Physical -- Dermatological Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Direct contact with mercaptans can result in skin irritation and chemical burns. These effects can occur when mercaptans contact the skin or through exposure to contaminated clothing or equipment.

retrieved_docs.title: Deadly Single Dose Agents -- Function -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Terpenoid (camphor): topical rubefacient induces local hyperemia and warmth

retrieved_docs.title: Bleach Toxicity(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Bleach exposure may occur by numerous mechanisms. Knowing these mechanisms is crucial to treatment decisions. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Sunscreens and Photoprotection -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: In vivo and in vitro animal studies have shown multiple potential adverse effects of UV filters within sunscreen. These adverse effects include endocrine dysfunction of the reproductive and developmental systems. The UV filters of concern include BP-3, 4-methylbenzylidene camphor (4-MBC), and OMC. An anti-estrogenic effect was strongly associated with BP-3, 3-benzylidene camphor (3-BC), 4-MBC, and OMC. This finding was based on the uterine weight of immature rats. UV filters demonstrated the anti-estrogenic effect of HMS, OD-PABA, and PABA in yeast expressing the human estrogen receptor α. Exposure to UV filters, including 3-BC and 4-MBC, resulted in delayed male puberty and reduced prostate weight. The mechanism of action behind the reproductive toxicity may be due to alterations in proteins of the gene expression of estrogen receptor, androgen receptor, progesterone receptor, insulin-like growth factor I, complement proteins, nuclear receptor co-repressor, and steroid receptor coactivator 1 in the uterus and prostate. In a 90-day study on BP-3, fertility was affected in male mice. This finding was demonstrated by decreased sperm density in a dose-related manner following dermal exposure in mice and oral exposure in mice and rats. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Euphorbia tirucalli Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Physical exam of the skin may reveal localized erythema, tenderness, blisters, vesicles, purpura, plaques, hemorrhagic bullae, or ulceration. [2] Findings may resemble a partial thickness burn, and affected areas will likely be tender to touch. Phytodermatitis findings are likely to occur in areas directly contacting the plant, most often the hands or fingers.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymorphic Light Eruption -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Management requires determining what provokes the eruption and then attempting to minimize this, while at the same time gradually increasing exposure to induce hardening.

retrieved_docs.title: Photodynamic Therapy for the Eye -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: After exposure to light of appropriate wavelength (470-700 nm), the photosensitizing agent undergoes biochemical changes leading to the generation of reactive oxygen species, which then results in selective cytotoxic damage.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Transmitted Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Synthetic Psoralen
retrieved_docs.content: Synthetic psoralen stops the multiplication of nearly all currently screened for pathogens. After exposure to ultraviolet (UV) light, synthetic psoralen forms cross-links in the nucleic acid chains. Synthetic psoralen does decrease platelet activity, requiring more frequent platelet transfusions. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2074
retrieved_docs.content: Hexyl 5‑aminolevulinate is classified as a photosensitizing agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: silymarin, spironolactone and other protective agents. Isolation and purification procedures are deemed appropriate for protective agents, promethazine, spironolactone, succinimides, tiotropium bromide, clorazepate dipotassium, amlodipine‑valsartan, penicillins, permethrin and silymarin. Toxicity assessment is required for N‑methyl‑scopolamine, serum albumins, arginine vasopressin, β‑carotene, chlortetracycline, chromonar, desonid, desoximetasone, dienestrol and dikumarol. The same agents are recognized as having notable toxic potential, capable of producing organ‑specific adverse effects, dose‑dependent reactions, and clinically significant drug‑drug interactions; consequently, careful dosing, vigilant monitoring and risk‑benefit evaluation are essential to prevent patient harm. In addition, these compounds function as “allowed qualifiers” in the context of ionizing radiation because they can modify biological responses to radiation—either enhancing radiation‑induced damage, altering

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: these compounds function as “allowed qualifiers” in the context of ionizing radiation because they can modify biological responses to radiation—either enhancing radiation‑induced damage, altering tissue radiosensitivity, or providing radioprotective or radiosensitizing effects. Awareness of these dual roles informs the integration of the agents into diagnostic imaging, radiotherapy planning, occupational radiation safety and broader radioprotection strategies, thereby optimizing therapeutic outcomes and patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1967
retrieved_docs.content: Hexyl 5-aminolevulinate is listed within the NCIt Antineoplastic Agent Terminology, indicating its classification as an antineoplastic (cancer‑treating) agent. At the same time, hexyl 5-aminolevulinate is a member of the class of fotosenzibilizující látky (photosensitizing agents), reflecting its role as a photosensitizer. This dual categorization highlights its relevance in oncology, particularly in photodynamic therapy where the compound is used to target and destroy malignant cells after activation by light, thereby integrating chemical antineoplastic properties with light‑induced cytotoxic effects for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2682
retrieved_docs.content: 4‑aminopyridin, beta‑karoten, buserelin, ochranné látky (protective substances), oxandrolon and urologické látky (urological agents) are all identified as relevant qualifiers for účinky záření (radiation effects). In medical contexts these compounds are recognized for their association with the biological impact of ionising radiation, either as agents that modify, protect against, or otherwise influence radiation‑induced outcomes. Their inclusion as qualifiers underscores the importance of considering such substances when evaluating or managing the effects of radiation exposure in patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2736
retrieved_docs.content: Akarbóza, anthralin, benzoyl peroxide, dienestrol, difenoxylát, griseofulvin, merkaptopurin, and tromethamin are all contraindicated in patients who have drug hypersensitivity (فرط الحساسية الدوائية). This means that individuals with a known allergy or hypersensitivity to medications should not be prescribed any of these agents, as doing so could trigger adverse allergic reactions. Recognizing and respecting this contraindication is essential for clinicians to avoid drug‑induced hypersensitivity events and to ensure safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The community organizes a collection of pharmaceutical agents into an analogue/derivative group that includes 4‑aminopyridin, acepromazine, acyclovir, akamprosát, alprostadil, auranofin, benaktyzin, cinoxacin, diltiazem, etodolac, hexachlorofen, kaptopril, kodein, atrakurium, benzoyl peroxide, chlorotrianisen, cinacalcet, demethylchlortetracyklin, dienestrol and alglucerase (the latter also linked to a specific structural class). A second set, denoted by an “allowed qualifier,” comprises diethylstilbestyl, dikumarol, etretinate, floroglucinol, galopamil, imatinib mesylate, karmustin, mercaptopurine and oxandrolone, reflecting a shared regulatory or pharmacological status. The knowledge base flags a broad spectrum of these compounds—4‑aminopyridin, acyclovir, akamprosát, alglucerase, alprostadil, atrakurium, auranofin, benzoyl peroxide, chlorotrianisen, cinacalcet, cinoxacin, demethylchlortetracyklin, dienestrol, diethylstilbestyl, dikumarol, diltiazem, etretinate, imatinib mesylate,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
retrieved_docs.content: corticosteroid potency categories inform dermatologic therapy and how podophyllotoxin‑derived antineoplastic drugs such as etoposide and teniposid are classified, employed in specific chemotherapy protocols, and linked to their underlying mutagenic chemistry.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1360
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib mesylate, Etodolac, and Sodium selenate are each contraindicated in patients who have فرط الحساسية الدوائية (drug hypersensitivity). Because a known hypersensitivity reaction to medications can lead to severe adverse effects, these drugs should be avoided in individuals with such a history. Recognizing this contraindication is essential for clinicians when evaluating a patient’s allergy profile before initiating therapy with any of these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: orlistat and paclitaxel are identified as allowed qualifiers for harmful or toxic effects. In addition, pinacidil, polymyxins, iron saccharate, silymarin, spironolactone and thalidomide each possess therapeutic indications yet are associated with significant adverse effects, requiring clinicians to balance benefits against potential toxicity. Recognizing which compounds are designated for therapeutic use, which influence or are affected by ionizing radiation, and which carry potential adverse or toxic outcomes enables clinicians to select appropriate medications, anticipate side‑effects, manage radiation‑related risks, and optimize patient safety and treatment efficacy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1798
retrieved_docs.content: 4‑aminopyridin, atrakurium, benzoylperoxid, imatinib mesylát, and selenan sodný are all identified as drugs that are contraindicated in patients with drug hypersensitivity (فرط الحساسية الدوائية). This shared contraindication underscores the need to assess for a history of drug allergy before prescribing any of these agents, as exposure in hypersensitive individuals could precipitate severe allergic reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1578
retrieved_docs.content: conditions; penicillins are fundamental antibiotics; permethrin is used as an insecticide and topical antiparasitic; silymarin has hepatoprotective properties; and certain spiro‑compounds have defined therapeutic applications. The allowed qualifier is also linked to agents with notable adverse‑effect profiles, including protective substances, paclitaxel, penicillins, permethrin, silymarin, spiro‑compounds, sodium tetradecyl sulfate, thalidomide and hexachlorophen. Importantly, alkylating agents are classified as a contraindicated drug class for the immunosuppressant azathioprine, indicating that azathioprine should be avoided in patients receiving alkylating agents. Awareness of these toxic potentials, therapeutic indications, and class‑specific contraindications is essential for safe and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 128
retrieved_docs.content: hormone antagonist elagolix (and its sodium salt) contraindicated in affected patients. The network also integrates flavonoid and coenzyme Q10 chemistry. The bioflavonoid rutin, a member of the bioflavonoidy class, is an active component of capillary‑stabilizing bioflavonoid drugs and contributes to vascular‑protective therapy; it appears as an inactive ingredient in a titanium‑dioxide/zinc‑oxide sunscreen lotion. The coenzyme Q10 analogue ubichinon (derived from the parent compound benzochinony) is present in zinc‑pyrithione medicated shampoo. Both rutin and ubichinon are listed among the inactive ingredients of dermatologic products. Within the ATC classification, capillary‑stabilizing agents—including horse chestnut seed and rutin‑based formulations—are grouped under “Other capillary stabilizing agents.” Metal‑based compounds intersect the network through industrial and pharmaceutical contexts. Ferric hydroxide, an iron compound (železité sloučeniny), together with calcium

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2057
retrieved_docs.content: The product titled “TITANIUM DIOXIDE 0.036 g in 1 mL / ZINC OXIDE 0.0234 g in 1 mL TOPICAL LOTION [SOOTHE Daily Mineral Sunscreen]” contains the inactive ingredient rutin. Rutin is a flavonoid with antioxidant properties that is sometimes added to topical formulations to help reduce oxidative stress on the skin. While the sunscreen’s active mineral filters—titanium dioxide and zinc oxide—provide broad‑spectrum UV protection, the inclusion of rutin reflects a formulation approach aimed at offering additional skin‑benefiting effects beyond simple sun protection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2903
retrieved_docs.content: acid) possesses anti‑inflammatory and immunomodulatory properties; mersalyl is linked to immunotoxic effects; nikotinylalkohol can alter immune‑cell function; oxandrolone, permethrin and pinacidil are permitted qualifiers in immunological contexts. All of these agents are also permitted qualifiers for urinary testing, meaning they may be detected, reported, or quantified in urine specimens to support comprehensive drug‑screening and interpretation of excretion profiles. Moreover, many of the same compounds—including N‑methylskopolamin, acetylcysteine, akamprosát, allylestrenol, arginin vasopressin, auranofin, buserelin, cetylpyridinium, cinoxacin, desoximetason, diosmin, erythropoietin, guma karaya, indoramin and hexachlorofen—function as modulators of radiation‑induced damage, radioprotective agents, or substances whose activity is altered by ionizing radiation. Their taxonomic placement within the B‑vitamin family and the documented dosage ranges, formulation contexts, and contraindications provide a comprehensive view of their therapeutic relevance and safety considerations.
